item.title: 500 upvotes isn't half bad
item.content:encoded:
It's half of a thousand!

The first time I read this article I didn't have a wiki account, so I couldn't upvote it, but now I'm happy I was lucky enough to bring it to a milestone. 10/10!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one has been getting a lot of mentions in lore-related YouTube videos, so despite the criticisms some have made about the exhaustion of the Omega-entity trope, it seems to be one of the top SCPs.

Let's be real there is a *lot* of content on here and most major tropes have all been done several times; but making the entity a dead, sympathetic African American man from the dust bowl era was an interesting characterization choice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad you think so. Those last few lines are some of my favorites I've ever written here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That ending was genuinely emotional

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: you have successfully created the most powerful thing in all of existence, and you did a great job doing it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
lets get on basic functions theory here

not all infinites are equal,some infinites grow or advance ''faster'' than others

think of the function f1(x)=x vs f2(x)=x^2

if x=2,f1=2 and f2=4
if x=3,f1=3 and f2=9

you see that f2 grows faster
there fore

for x=infinite

f2 would grow infinitely faster than f1

so based on this
sure they might be universes were this anomaly doesnt exist,but they are exceptions,special cases,rather than rules
and even if they are infinite quantity of them,the rate at wich universes over this anomaly is present,would higher than those where it doesnt (like with f1 and f2)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For the first few entries I was stuck on the fact that this seemed like…basically a more technical explanation of 239. The XK scenario & the meta level the article is written on turned out really spectacular though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Something that bothers me about this, is that if SCP-3812 exists across all universes/ realities/ narratives at the same time, by definition, would there not be a different version of it out there, say one without schizophrenia or any of the other ailments it has? One where its perfectly healthy in both mind and body?

Along those same lines, would there not be an infinite amount of different versions of SCP-3812, where in one universe/ version, it was say, a white guy, an asian woman, or a completely different species, say like a fly, or even a whale?

If one being, "Ben" in this case I guess, existing within a higher level narrative universe could create 3812, or something similiar, could another existing on an even higher narrative universe do/ make something bigger?

Think of it like with levels of experience, "Ben" in this case, created the overall narrative design and being of 3812 using his experience and powers, etc. But if a being ABOVE his narrative universe created something like 3812, would that not instantanously eclipse 3812 purely by being made with a higher level narrative matierial and expierience, and thus having a higher degree of narrative fluency to work with?

Just curious is all.

ARRRRRRRGH. THIS SITE IS OBSESSED WITH MAKING MY MIND MELT.

Jokes aside, this is, as pointed out, an SCP that's already been done probably a dozen times over. But the thing that makes this SCP stand out is that, unlike all the other ones, it actually implies that there is not just anger or outright evil involved; rather, it comes across as something that is scared of what may happen, and is taking out its frustration of not knowing out on everyone/everything else. I was a little iffy on the whole thing, but that last transcript is what sold me. "It" found peace, and has learned to accept its fate. It reads out like a tale, to an extent, and while there's a lot of unknowns that still exist when it comes to this SCP, it has a nice conclusion that not very many SCPs get.

Hopefully I got the concept right, or I'll look stupid. Either way, it's well written, and it deserves more phrase than it's gotten so far. (Something like this should be cracking the +500's, but maybe that's just my two-cents.)

item.title: Re: Oversold
item.content:encoded:
~sigh~

I don't mean any offense. You're one of the most tremendously talented authors on this site

Please don't do this. This isn't really directed at you or anyone else, but this is something that gets on my nerves more than anything else people do here. If you're going to critique an article, do so on the merits of the article alone. Your first paragraph, while something I outright disagree with, is at least critique. Turning it around afterwards and saying "you're a good author but" is some of the most condescending shit.

What that amounts to is little more than "you can do better than this" which, frankly, is nobody's business. If I'm posting something to the wiki, it's because I think it's good. I don't write shite just to post it and hope people come rub my belly and tell me it's good. Saying what is essentially "you should know that this isn't good" is just so goddamn aggravating. I would hope that I, maybe more than anybody, would know what I think is good.

Don't do this, to anybody, but especially not to me. Christ.

Also,

In any case, the market is saturated with the idea, and it's time to let it go.

This article is +345 at the time of this posting, and is one of the more frequently discussed articles off-site. I don't really know what else to say about that.

item.title: Oversold
item.content:encoded:
This one is so oversold it loses any punch it could have had. The declaration of this being "way magical, but it's magic is magicaler than other magic, and also by the way more dangerous than any danger" kills the entry dead. The intro description claims it to be impossible to describe in any meaningful way, and then goes on to describe it with no real ambiguity at all. It's a madman that can do whatever it wants and can't be destroyed, and infinite power is not interesting at all. There are also at least eight SCPs that have already done the whole "this thing lives in a higher reality and writes our story", including, notably, two 001 proposals. -1… Unless I'm missing something and there was a contest or something built specifically around that concept. In any case, the market is saturated with the idea, and it's time to let it go.

I don't mean any offense. You're one of the most tremendously talented authors on this site, and of the thousand or so articles I've read there are at least a couple in my top ten. But they're good because of the combination of your creativity and writing skill, and cribbing this concept and just making it bigger (more mysteriously magical, more powerful than all-powerful) doesn't compare to your other works and just oversaturates the pataphysics conceptual space even more than it already is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly it was awesome for me (I love breaches in the fourth wall and higher-dimensional shit) but the "hey I trascend the writer even though I'm a fictional character in a website" really breaks suspension of disbelief and immersion.

item.title: OH GOD OH FUCK
item.content:encoded: OH GOD OH FUCK

item.title: Re: Thoughts on 3812
item.content:encoded:
slight necro, but i love the way you respond to criticisms like this.

please continue being an awesome writer forever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: guys this scp is real i have the proof

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: accidental, disregard

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So… Could 3812 survive to 2935?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely a +1, the ending funnily enough reminded me of the end credits of Minecraft

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think there is a really respectable compositional move here which is the casting of reality-bending as pataphysics. That's one of those connect-the-dot moves that makes you say "why didn't I think of that?" However, I can't get behind the actual anomaly or the treatment of it. It's an X-Man/Mary Sue turned up to 11, finally tempered at the precipice by solipsism that (personally!) doesn't have the philosophical originality or penetrance that it probably should in order to get the job done, per the set-up of this individual's highly distorted and psychotic mind.

item.title: Re: Thoughts on 3812
item.content:encoded: Yeah I think that's where this strains disbelief suspension to the snapping point for me. We are told this transcends Kactus but that's obviously not really the case, which just reminds me that this is fiction. I can't help but think of the Adventure Time episode where the Ice King tries to summon Fiona and Cake by writing that they found a portal to the Ice King's world, and of course this doesn't work.

item.title: Re: Thoughts on 3812
item.content:encoded:
I agree with this post. Defining the realities as "narratives" stacked infinitely is interesting sure, but it's hard for me as a reader to take these ideas too seriously, as, at the end of the day, I am still reading a story on the internet.
I'm not convinced that I have encountered a god because I am still reading about him on a website for fictional tales.
Most meta SCPs have a way of cleverly acknowledging their medium, but this one seems in direct conflict with its.
No vote for me

item.title: Re: Thoughts on 3812
item.content:encoded: ok

item.title: Thoughts on 3812
item.content:encoded: Personally, I thought it was a fun concept for a story, not horrible but a little generic…. but that would be for a story, in my mind and opinion, SCP's are supposed to be more than stories, ideas, and concepts of things that don't follow our ideas and concepts. But this is just a completely omnipotent being, it throws the almighty out the window, 239, 343, the Crimson Khan, Mekhane, Yaldabaoth, the hanged king, every single SCP we thought was the pinnacle gets one-upped eventually, and that's fine by me, but this is just the one-upped SCP of all. Its one-upping itself. I liked the little bits of conversation between Scranton and his audience and the Dr but this thing, it closes off all other SCP's that wanted to be super powerful, because you just look at this and it's stronger because it surpasses all! This may be intentional to attempt to stop SCP's like that but I feel it shouldn't have been stopped, I personally love the eldritch abominations and incomprehensible gods because it lets me get invested in a story, but when things like this pop up, trying to be the eldritch abomination of eldritch abominations of eldritch abominations… it just makes it seems bad to me, I downvoted and I doubt anyone can change my mind because of all the reasons I listed, you're welcome to try to convince me and I will listen but that's why I downvoted And personally don't consider it canon

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's pretty funny that you feel that many people won't like this one. Of everything you have written, this is the one I love most of all.

Meta pieces are really overdone. But you somehow managed to make it feel fresh again.

+1. If I could give you +2, I would.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Image Sources
– hide block

unshaped.jpg
unshaped2.jpg

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Show Block
- Close

AgentWilson nailed it for me. Humes and SRAs are a good concept- whether the devices are used to contain a given anomaly or not, their existence gives us the idea that the Foundation as an organization does more than secure/contain/protect us from individual anomalies. They learn from the work, look for patterns across multiple anomalies, and develop a body of knowledge that produces defenses applicable to more than one kind of threat. Almost since the beginning, a major theme of the site has been "the Foundation does bad things to maintain normality, but it's better than the alternative"- to the point where, if you read enough articles with that theme in a row, you wonder whether those scales really balance out anymore. Why not just destroy dangerous anomalies, or release benign ones to the world? Why not go to organizations that aren't in on the masquerade for help with containment? Are we sure the Foundation works? Kant counters, Scranton Boxes, and Reality Anchors reassure us that it does. The Foundation has the tools to contain dangerous anomalies that no one else can, thanks to the breadth and depth of experience gained from securing thousands of these things and finding ways to contain them with their effects intact. Blowing up an anomalous object and declaring victory might reduce the number of anomalies in the world, but it would never provide any knowledge of how to deal with an anomaly that resists explosives.

But an anomaly that by all measure should be subject to those tools and isn't is a good idea too, because it refutes the idea that SRAs can fix anything and reality is basically safe. This is a horror franchise. We can't and shouldn't give our protagonists perfect weaponry, because then the only thing left to be afraid of is them1. So we need things like SCP-1943, SCP-3557, and this guy, to expand the category of threats that the Foundation faces and push back the boundaries of what exists beyond the frontier of what is understood.

(Incidentally, I really appreciated your explanation of what Humes actually measure in scientific terms. It's easy to think of "reality" as "phenomena that are not anomalous" if you don't think about it too hard… but if you're reading about Humes, you're probably thinking hard enough to notice the glaring flaw2. Defining the quantity of "reality" as "how consistent are the laws of physics here?" provides a more concrete understanding without losing simplicity. A universe where gravity is stronger per unit mass than magnetism may be extremely different from our own, but as long as the gravitational constant is still a constant, it shouldn't be considered a "less real" place. "Less real" should only indicate conditions where some physical laws don't apply to everything equally, allowing for anomalies.)

I would be willing to upvote based on the execution of the basic concept alone. You never really characterize the SCP at first, but you establish him as this existential threat that fulfills a necessary role in the mythos, and you weave a whole narrative around him that ties into the basic concept of the Foundation as well as the backstory of several other SCPs and prominent characters.

And then you drop in the last addendum, which DOES characterize the SCP, and even gives him an arc, taking him from the terrifying unknown to the terrifyingly malicious to the awe-inspiringly transcendent in the space of a couple of pages. You even address one of the fridge logic questions posed by a lot of many-worlds-theory fiction: "if there are infinite timelines containing everything that can possibly exist, shouldn't there be entities who can travel from one timeline to another, and shouldn't some of them have visited the reality where I'm reading this?".

Excellent work. An enthusiastic +1.

Please collapse long posts ~ Leveritas

Footnotes
1. Which is all right if you want to embrace it, but essentially just puts us back at stage 2: "are we sure the Foundation are the good guys?".
2. "Wait, what does 'anomalous' mean besides 'phenomena we don't see in reality'? How would you define it if you lived in a universe where anomalies are real?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know meta pieces are more and more common these days, but I still love the emotional boldness of putting something of the core of yourself as a writer on the page. I like the instinct which drives it. And I think this concept honestly wouldn't work at all without the meta element — and it's followed all the way to its logical thematic conclusion (somewhat eschewing real-world logic to do so, which I approve of), rather than shying away with a vague addendum at the last moment.

I also love how it was written. I love the emotion put into the end. The style and the authorial reference aspect reminds me of SCP-3999 and [another kaktus SCP I can't mention without spoilers].

+1. I started out hating this, but I've already come back to reread the ending several times over the course of the three weeks since I read it.

Good title, too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't want to hate this, but I can't really bring myself to enjoy it more. I like the basic premise (especially the metafictional elements), and the concept of what being a god would truly mean is explained well, but the way the article runs away with its ideas leads me to generally dislike it.

The Foundation basically said "We can't really contain this god, so let's just observe it and stop any problems that crop up." While that itself is fine, if not rather uninteresting, it also allows for wave after wave of exposition and narrative, eventually leading to the article reading like a tale or other kind of article forced to fit into SCP format. Exploring cool ideas is cool, but here it was done badly, I feel.

Also, I'm not sure that creating a god is possible in the way described. Technically, every author can warp the reality of the stories they write. However, no normal author can create a character that has influence outside the frame of its own narrative, and I have trouble visualizing even an anomalous author creating a character that can influence literally all realities.

-1 from me. :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think what really sets this guy apart from other reality-benders is his self-awareness - in a sense. What I mean by that is a lot of all the other reality-benders think they've gotten it 'sorted out', in that they know their purpose. However, just like human beings, reality benders are also meaningless individuals. This guy, on the other hand, recognises he is a meaningless individual over an equally meaningless series of realities.

After realising that, he becomes just a bit more human to me.

+1, a brilliant article.

item.title: Hey. Well done.
item.content:encoded:
I was sleepy and restless when I first started reading this - you know how it gets some days. I am now aware and awake, and there was a stillness in my heart as I read the last parts of this. The stillness of watching something important happening - even if it's something personal, like something important to the author.
I get the feeling it is, at least in some certain ways.

It doesn't help that it jives with a lot of my own philosophy and personal experiences. I'd call it nihilism but…that word has so much bad baggage on it, in common usage. It's more than just "there is no meaning to anything", even if that is the lynchpin of it.

But that ascension, towards the end, that "enlightenment" in that final conversation where 3812 begins learning to let go of…all of it - while also learning that doing harm is needless. It really struck a chord.

So I guess it was important to me, too.
Maybe that's why I found myself finding it really, incredibly beautiful.

The last part where 3812 and 3812 acknowledge and talk about you does seem heavy-handed. But the point of it all is that all this is bigger than anything anyways, right? Yourself included. Something like that.

Either way - I really appreciated this. Kant once espoused that the true mark of fine art is it's ability to stir contemplation in it's viewer. It certainly has done that. Nicely done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see him. A man at a keyboard. He’s watching this right now.

This feels like tipping your hand just a little too much, especially when you've toyed with this concept throughout the entire piece. Even the message in addendum 5 was a little bit much.

That aside, I really adore this piece. I can't agree at all with the critiques that this is too broad or overarching — one of the beautiful things about this site is that it's able to accommodate concepts as ambitious as this one and as specific as some of the Series I reality benders. Maybe the narrative could be tightened here or compressed there, but that's a separate critique.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1
Screw the fourth wall, we never needed it anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's pretty engaging as far as the writing goes, which is impressive considering it's length. But the idea is just kind of boring. Godly reality-bender that uses a meta aspect as it's sole interesting point just doesn't click much, to me. It's just an extremely overpowered average dudenomaly that happens to understand the nature of its reality, which doesn't go that far in saving it from being a really boring concept.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I honestly love this, this breaks almost all the rules that the site has pertaining to writing OTT and OP SCPs.

+1 with love.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

Grammatical/Pedantic Notes:

It’s not user error; we’ve poured over and studied thousands of hours of logs

"Poured" should probably be "pored"

Since SCP-3812 hasn’t doing that

Missing a word here

Due to the nature of these alterations, the likelihood of the event having actually ever taken place is listed

The likelihood actually isn't listed in this table

Eventually, the vaguely humanoid shape of SCP-3812 appeared again within the star. SCP-3812 began a long series of vocalizations, apparently a conversation with itself, the entirety of which was recorded by an exposed Foundation deep-sea microphone in the area.

This could be justified with more meta screwery, but a star that burns so hot that the atmosphere is ignited should not in any way be able to be heard by a microphone nearby, for a laundry list of reasons. For one, there wouldn't be any medium on which the sound could reach the microphone from the center of a star.

I'm iffy on this. For the non-shortened version, refer to the giant collapsible below.

+ show block
– hide block

The article approaches the meta subject with a great deal of seriousness, jargon, and explicit explanations. By the time I'm finished I feel like I've experienced at least three layers of nested meta-explanations where we're having an entity expositionally converse about the expositional letter that gave context to the expositional dry books that gave context to the expositional staff memos, with expositional footnotes scattered throughout.

It's not a very lean read. It seems like there's a lot of stuff here that isn't really necessary to tell the story and so it becomes a slog through repeated explanations and contextualizing and heavy-handedness. There's a huge amount of space dedicated to talking about how darn bad this anomaly is, and other massive blocks of text dedicated to metaphysical technobabble that by the end just confuses.

It seems to me like meta articles need to strike a careful balance. Articles that play with meta for humor, like Operation ÓverMeta and Murphy Law series benefit from being blatant and whimsical with the idea, while more serious interpretations intending drama or horror, like SCP-2747 or Swann's Proposal, tend to keep their meta aspects hidden until close to the end. In this article, almost exactly 25% into the considerable wordcount, you come out and explicitly define the metaphysical foundation of the article.

From there, the cat is out of the bag, so you're left with what you can explicitly define. There's some interesting imagery used to describe the metafictional stuff going on here (I liked the tower analogy) but it's unclear to what extent things are really happening. The article builds up the idea of metafictional layers colliding together, the difficult bits being erased entirely and considering realities that aren't just below our own but above our own as well. That's some cool stuff that isn't commonly explored. But, at the same time, it isn't really all that explored here: the final log describes what is to all appearances an XK-class event localized to the Foundation's universe, and while the dialogue seems to imply this is intentional, it's a different, less interesting idea than that being built up for the whole article.

Based on what else is in the article, it feels a bit convenient that what happens here is possible at all, that 3812 becomes lucid(ish) at the best possible moment, that fictional!non-fictional!Ben tries hitting 3812 with big sticks and then nothing else (or, heck, using his absolute power in universe to give the Foundation or humanity any kind of advantage), and that 3812 only wants to hurt this one specific universe because apparently at some point it became a question of intent and not a lack of comprehension, as was indicated for the rest of the article.

I want to like this, and there are good moments, but it has a lot of serious issues.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Image source:

https://www.flickr.com/photos/29324474@N02/5351688267

Will update shortly with other images.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like bits of this. The images that introduce the article I thought were a great touch and started off strong.

However, I don't like where this went. Specifically it tries too hard to top the other reality benders, to the point of claiming origin, which is a strong dislike for me. SCP's that make other SCP's, especially ones as storied and entrenched as the Little Witch, really compound this issue. I am not more afraid of or interested in the skip, I am simply less impressed by the others, and that doesn't add anything.

Additionally, Meta-fiction is best when it's subtle and unsettling, whereas this peers through the fourth wall a bit too hard to take it seriously in-universe.

A reluctant -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've always been fascinated by the nature of fiction and the whole concept of realities existing in some sort of meta hierarchy, and likewise I've always been drawn to works of art (not just SCP entries, but especially so for them) where it really feels like the author poured their soul into what they were making, and that they legitimately needed to make it for the sake of their own sanity. This combines both of those things and kept me hooked throughout, and while I may have had minor issues with a handful of things throughout the article, it spoke to me far too much for me to consider giving it anything other than an upvote.

I don't know if this means anything to you, but even though I do stop by here every once in a while to read some articles and chat in site19, I rarely log in to upvote articles, and even more rarely do I leave comments on them. The last article that spoke to me enough to get me to log in and leave a comment was SCP-3999, so I guess what I'm trying to say is that this article is pretty special, at least to me. Make of that what you will.

item.title: I liked it but I dwell on the smallest things.
item.content:encoded:
SCP-239 is mentioned in this, if you take the 8 and the 1 in 3812 and put them together you get 9, then with 392, you rearrange the numbers and you get 239.
Am I going to deep in this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay. I'm more relaxed now. My name happens to be Ben so I freaked out for a second there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Absolutely loved it, I was completely entranced the whole way through.
Keep up the good work :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are a number of things about this that aren’t entirely to my taste, but the final addendum does a much better job in reaching for something transcendent than the previous version.

In fact, I think I’d be at an upvote if there was some way you could convince me that the first, deleted posting was a deliberate part of the skip’s meta-narrative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not really fazed by the idea of the ultimate god of the universe being The Girl Reading This

i can't sttop laughing at this, you genius.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After reading S. Andrew Swann's 001 Proposal and some other articles I forgot about, I'm not really fazed by the idea of the ultimate god of the universe being The Girl Reading This (or the authors, whatever).
Still, highly enjoyed reading this. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for overall quality, but I have a Schrodinger's opinion on the concept. I never liked the whole Hume/SRA thing because it seemed like too much of a cop out and removed some of the mystique from reality benders, so whenever a "bigger fish" comes along I tend to like it, but the twist at the end made it feel too meta for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The narrative hierarchy idea was interesting enough to me to initially upvote midway through, but after finishing it I had to switch to a downvote. There two big problems are that it's not scary, in any sense of the word, and that a key part of the story makes no sense whatsoever.

Yes, 3812 could destroy reality. But we can't possibly do anything about that or meaningfully interact with him in any way, so that's about as scary as "what if our reality ends because physics shifts to a different metastable state."

The bigger problem is that the creation of 3812 only works if we don't think to much about what "supercede" actually means. In the context of the narrative there are two plausible definitions for what it means if we say "A superceeds B":

  • A can edit B's narrative, or
  • A can edit B's narrative and B can't edit A's narrative.

The first one means that 3812 simply ends up being able to edit himself (along with everything else, of course). That leads to the obvious solution of editing himself away, or back to a normal human, or anything that isn't super-omnipotent.
The second definition reduces your grand paradox to "if P then not-P". Congrats, you've rediscovered the ability to prove anything from a contradiction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What a wild ride that was…

I almost gave up reading early on, but I'm glad I didn't.

I can't say I love all of it; the whole "almighty reality bender" thing put me off and felt somewhat generic. At least at first.

It also feels somewhat clunky at times, though I'd be hard-pressed to say where, exactly. More of a general feeling, and I can easily excuse that given the scale.

Something kept me going, though, and the more I read, the more I liked it.

I'd love to be able to give some clear, concise and critical feedback, but honestly, I think I'm just a bit too overwhelmed for that.

Ultimately, I think it's the the meta that wraps it all up nicely for me. The fact that it "resonates" with my personal philosophy certainly doesn't hurt.

There's a lot of overlap, here (all personal opinion, of course): it could be a -J, it could be an 001 proposal, it works as mainlist, the meta's kind of a pseudo-format screw… I don't know. There's just so much going on.

Almost too much. Almost.

In a way, reading this entry kind of feels a bit like (a pale version of) what SCP-3812 is going through. There's an overload of information, but in the end, I'm left feeling that the whole is greater than the sum of its parts.

So… thank you for that.

Easy +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm.I think the "correspondence" in the second "BY OTDER" picture is spelt wrong.
By the way,if only with level 5 classification can I view the document,why would some addendums still need the same classification?
And,the XK occured in 2016 or 2017? Glad to see it was fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cool. Dunno why we have to have this conversation here but ok.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
However, that tends to fall down when the concept you're working with is rather thin, which is frequently is.

This is an issue that I've had with a lot of Series 4 articles, including this one. I would take a compact article with a bizarre and fleshed out idea any day, over an article overly long narrative with a weak concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nothing too surprising here, I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been a little sniffy about your writing in the past, and I think this article perfectly encapsulates my problem with your work. You are an excellent writer- among the best of the wiki- who writes with a lot of skill, subtlety and intricacy. You have a detailed and expansive style which can work excellently.

However, that tends to fall down when the concept you're working with is rather thin, which is frequently is. It's why I dislike 3000 and was so ambivalent towards your Assyrian god skip. Those were both rather basic concepts which couldn't hold the strain of a lot of detailed prose. 3000 ended up being a lot of waffle on a very basic theme, running around the same circles and moving glacially. The Assyrian god skip was mildly amusing and extended far too long. This is in stark contrast to some of your others like O, Death or the Lonely Liar, where the repetition of the scale of depth and the gradual unveiling of the deception of Pan Hun create a marvellous atmosphere in each respectively.

This one, however, is a thin concept which consistent repetitive elaboration does little to alleviate. At its core, this isn't very interesting. We've had better articles dealing with interlocking narratives and hierarchies of narratives. We've had better meta articles, better reality-bender articles, and so on. You synthesise a lot of these things together, but it's pretty obvious where you're going from the get-go and nothing really changes. There's nothing particularly innovating in the style, such as in 3999, and you don't use trickery and allusion like in 2747; you just have a very straight story about what has now become rather traditional fare for the wiki.

There is some good stuff here. You build up a nicely creepy atmosphere with the mysterious resurrection, and the limited exposure we get to the human before the final addendum is a nice piece of buildup. And I like a lot of the conversation in that addendum. But it's played too safe in terms of structure and style, without adding anything other than a spin on what's already been done.

-1, with regret. (God knows I'm guilty of just the same cliches/problems in my own stuff, of course; you once pointed out, quite correctly, how derivative my 001 about the expanding city was of other apocalyptic skips.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't really understand why, but entities like this somehow give me a chill, just something about the concept of infinite layers of reality and this thing being forced to experience it all. just chills me.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's back, and I love it. Everyone else already said everything I wanted to say.

+1 hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I downvoted it; I don't know the usual rules of voting, so if this isn't the way it's supposed to work, let me know and I'll change my vote.

If you didn't enjoy the read, go ahead and downvote. If someone pressures you to change your vote even if you didn't enjoy the read, notify a staff member.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: dammit, the others took all my words I wanted to say… take my +1 and my explanation is for the reasons above
